InVivo Atlas Segmentation Examples¶

Example 1: Validation of Single Image and Segment¶

We generated 4, 7x7x7 voxel cubes within 3 segments: 1 in ACA, 1 in CP, and 2 in PRN. For all cubes, voxel intensity values are as follows: Center voxel equal is to 4; Inner shell = 3; Middle shell = 2; and Outermost shell = 1. This is summarized in the following table.

Segment/ROI #Voxels Intensity
ACA 1 4
ACA 26 3
ACA 98 2
ACA 218 1
CP 1 4
CP 26 3
CP 98 2
CP 218 1
PRN 2 4
PRN 52 3
PRN 193 2
PRN 436 1

InVivo Atlas segmentation of these cubes at varying intensity thresholds of >0, >1 or >2 will result in the following statistical summaries.

Segment/ROI Threshold MEAN MEDIAN StDev Q1 Q3 MIN MAX ActVol FAV CoGx CoGy CoGz
ACA > 0 1.45 1.00 0.65 1.00 2.00 1.00 4.00 343 0.067 62.0 71.0 60.0
ACA > 1 2.22 2.00 0.44 2.00 2.00 2.00 4.00 125 0.024 62.0 71.0 60.0
ACA > 2 3.04 3.00 0.19 3.00 3.00 3.00 4.00 27 0.005 62.0 71.0 60.0
CP > 0 1.45 1.00 0.65 1.00 2.00 1.00 4.00 343 0.015 42.0 63.0 32.0
CP > 1 2.22 2.00 0.44 2.00 2.00 2.00 4.00 125 0.006 42.0 63.0 32.0
CP > 2 3.04 3.00 0.19 3.00 3.00 3.00 4.00 27 0.001 42.0 53.0 32.0
PRN > 0 1.45 1.00 0.65 1.00 2.00 1.00 4.00 686 0.119 63.5 115.5 26.0
PRN > 1 2.22 2.00 0.44 2.00 2.00 2.00 4.00 250 0.043 63.5 115.5 26.0
PRN > 2 3.04 3.00 0.19 3.00 3.00 3.00 4.00 54 0.009 63.5 115.5 26.0

These numbers/tables and manual calculations can be found in */examples/validation/ValidationDataComparison.xlsx.

Below we show an example of how you would run the segmentation gui in a terminal/command line window using the inputs/outputs in /examples/atlas and /examples/validation directories. We display the resultant segmentation dataframes as a table to compare to the manual calculations above (from ValidationDataComparison.xlsx). Lastly, we show example column graph summaries from the MultisiteValidation.nii segmentation using the Intensities > 0 threshold.

Imports and Setup¶

In [3]:
# Imports for all Python code chunks
## these will need to be imported any time you run a similar analysis

# Import InVivoSegment Plotting Module
from scripts.invivo_plot import matplotlib_columngraph as plt

# Import Standard Python Libraries for File and Data Handling
import os
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as mplt

# Set segments to remove - see InVivo Atlas lookup table for details
seg_to_remove = ["WB","VL","V3","aot","opt","onl","SEZ","aco","em","cc","int","fi","vhc","st","sm","Gr"]

# Define Dimensional Scaling Function for Graph Size
def dim_scale(tpl, scalar):
    return tuple(x*scalar for x in tpl)

Running Segmentation GUI¶

You can run the segmentation GUI in a jupyter notebook as shown in the code chunk below (uncommented - remove first '#' on each line). However, we recommend that you run the segmentation directly in a terminal window. Note that we run the segmentation on the MultisiteValidation.nii multiple times generating multiple output segmentation csv files, one for each threshold used (>0, >1, and >2).

In [ ]:
## Run GUI for MultisiteValidation.nii
#!python ./InVivoSegment.py --mode gui

Table Summary¶

In [4]:
for thr in (0,1,2):
    if thr == 0:
        validation_df = pd.read_csv(("./examples/validation/OutputData/CSVs/ValidationResults_thr" + str(thr) + ".csv"), na_values=["", " ", "NA", "-"])
    else: 
        validation_df_tmp = pd.read_csv(("./examples/validation/OutputData/CSVs/ValidationResults_thr" + str(thr) + ".csv"), na_values=["", " ", "NA", "-"])
        validation_df = pd.concat([validation_df, validation_df_tmp])

# Print Table as Assess Difference from Manual Calculations (Excel)
valdf_tb = validation_df.loc[validation_df['SegAbbr'].isin(["ACA","CP","PRN"]),['SegAbbr','Threshold',"Mean","Median","StDev","Q1","Q3","Min","Max","ActVol","FAV","sCoGx","sCoGy","sCoGz"]].sort_values(by=['SegAbbr', 'Threshold'], ascending=[True, True])

html_string_no_index = valdf_tb.to_html(index=False)

# To display this raw string as a table in Jupyter:
from IPython.display import HTML
HTML(html_string_no_index)
Out[4]:
SegAbbr Threshold Mean Median StDev Q1 Q3 Min Max ActVol FAV sCoGx sCoGy sCoGz
ACA 0.0 1.45 1.0 0.65 1.0 2.0 1.0 4.0 343 0.067 62.0 71.0 60.0
ACA 1.0 2.22 2.0 0.44 2.0 2.0 2.0 4.0 125 0.024 62.0 71.0 60.0
ACA 2.0 3.04 3.0 0.19 3.0 3.0 3.0 4.0 27 0.005 62.0 71.0 60.0
CP 0.0 1.45 1.0 0.65 1.0 2.0 1.0 4.0 343 0.015 42.0 63.0 32.0
CP 1.0 2.22 2.0 0.44 2.0 2.0 2.0 4.0 125 0.006 42.0 63.0 32.0
CP 2.0 3.04 3.0 0.19 3.0 3.0 3.0 4.0 27 0.001 42.0 63.0 32.0
PRN 0.0 1.45 1.0 0.65 1.0 2.0 1.0 4.0 686 0.119 63.5 115.5 26.0
PRN 1.0 2.22 2.0 0.44 2.0 2.0 2.0 4.0 250 0.043 63.5 115.5 26.0
PRN 2.0 3.04 3.0 0.19 3.0 3.0 3.0 4.0 54 0.009 63.5 115.5 26.0

Summary: Comparing the segmentation-generated table to the manual calculcations above we find identical numbers, indicating accurate segmentation. We recommend you run this segmentation, and those of below to validate your segmentation procedure before running on your own data.

Column Graph Summaries¶

In [36]:
# Plot Column Graph Summaries for various metrics
# Define threshold >0 subset for plotting
validation_df_subset = validation_df.loc[validation_df['Threshold'] == 0,]
for val in ("SegVol", "Mean", "Median", "StDev", "Q1", "Q3", "Min", "Max", "ActVol", "FAV", "sCoGx", "sCoGy", "sCoGz"):
    # Define plot names for saving 
    plot_name = os.path.join("./examples/validation/OutputData/ColumnGraphs",("Validation_" + val + "_thr0.png"))
    ax = plt(
        seg_df = validation_df_subset,
        seg_rm = seg_to_remove,
        order = "Grouping", 
        y = val,
        fill = "Group",
        col_vals = ("#E34234", "#0000FF",
                    "#FFFF00", "#87CEEB"),
        save = True,
        fname = plot_name,
        fig_dims = dim_scale((3.3, 1.1), 2),
        font_scale = 2
        )

Summary: As observed in the above plots, all segments have been segmented as indicated by the SegVol graph showing the segment volumes, i.e., number of voxels (log10). Segments are ordered to the InVivo Atlas convention, by domain and then segment within domain, roughly anterior to posterior and superficial to deep. For instance, from left to right is olfactory regions then cortex, then deeper telencephalon regions, then diencephalon regions, then mes/met/myelencephalon and lastly cerebellum.

Note that the graphs indicate that the correct/expected statistics have been calculated by our segmentation process for the Anterior Cingulate Area (ACA in CTX). Furthermore, all regions without a validation cube (i.e., all other segments) have values of 0 for all segmental measures.

Example 2: Segmentation of Simulated Noise-only (Negatives) Controls¶

For this example we have a statistical (Cohen's D effect size) map corresponding to a voxel-wise paired t-test that compared a set of simulated images with only voxel-wise noise. The statistical map comparing 'noise-only' simulated data was generated in SPM12 using the optimal smoothing kernel, t-value threshold and cluster extent (see paper for details: n=11; kernel = 150 $\mu m$; T(10), p<0.05 $\ge$ 1.81; cluster extent $\ge$ 8 voxels). Suprathreshold voxels in this map reflect False Positives from dataset noise.

The goal of segmenting this statistical map of false positives is to obtain distributions noise-driven magnitudes of segmental measures to compare against real data. This analysis can be used for selecting a lower bound threshold for various segmentation statistics.

In [ ]:
# Load SPM segmentation CSV
noise_df = pd.read_csv("./examples/noise_simulations/OutputData/CSVs/NoiseSegmentationResults.csv", na_values=["", " ", "NA", "-"])

noise_df['Group'] = noise_df['Group'].astype('category')
# Tranform a few variables
## Center of Gravity of Signal from Segmental CoG
noise_df['Euc Dist']  = np.sqrt((noise_df['sCoGx']-noise_df['CoGx'])**2 + 
                              (noise_df['sCoGx']-noise_df['CoGx'])**2 + 
                              (noise_df['sCoGz']-noise_df['CoGz'])**2) * 0.1 
noise_df['Euc Distyz']  = np.sqrt((noise_df['sCoGx']-noise_df['CoGx'])**2 +
                                  (noise_df['sCoGz']-noise_df['CoGz'])**2) * 0.1 
noise_df['Euc Distx'] = (noise_df['sCoGx']-noise_df['CoGx']) * 0.1 
noise_df['Euc Disty'] = (noise_df['sCoGy']-noise_df['CoGy']) * 0.1 
noise_df['Euc Distz'] = (noise_df['sCoGz']-noise_df['CoGz']) * 0.1
# converted to mm
for index, row in noise_df.iterrows():
    if row["sCoGx"] == 0 and row["sCoGy"] == 0 and row["sCoGz"] == 0:
        noise_df.at[index,"Euc Dist"] = 0
## Convert T-value to Cohen's D
n = 11 # number of subjects
for val in ["Mean", "Median", "StDev", "Q1", "Q3", "Min", "Max"]:
    noise_df[val]  = noise_df[val] / np.sqrt(n)

noise_df['COV CohD']   = noise_df['StDev'] / noise_df['Mean']
noise_df_tmp = noise_df.copy()
for val in ("FAV", "Mean", "COV CohD",
            "Euc Dist",'Euc Distyz', "Euc Distx", "Euc Disty", "Euc Distz"):
    # remove spaces in val names
    val2 = val.replace(" ", "-")
    plot_name = os.path.join("./examples/noise_simulations/OutputData/ColumnGraphs",("NoiseOnly_" + val2 + "_optimal.png"))
    ax = plt(
        seg_df = noise_df_tmp,
        seg_rm = seg_to_remove,
        order = "Grouping", 
        y = val,
        fill = "Condition",
        col_vals = ("#0000FF", "#87CEEB"),
        save = False,
        fname = plot_name,
        fig_dims = dim_scale((3.3, 1.1), 2),
        font_scale = 2
        )
    
    # Plot distributions
    fig, ax = mplt.subplots()
    
    noise_df_tmp2 = noise_df.loc[noise_df["FAV"] > 0]
    noise_df_tmp2[val].hist(bins=10, color="#0000FF", alpha = 0.5, edgecolor='black')
    # 3. Add vertical dotted lines at mean ± 3*std
    mean_val = noise_df_tmp2[val].mean()
    std_val = noise_df_tmp2[val].std()
    mplt.axvline(mean_val, color='black', linestyle='dotted', linewidth=2, label='Mean')
    mplt.axvline(mean_val + 3*std_val, color='0.4', linestyle='dotted', linewidth=2, label='+3 SD')
    mplt.axvline(mean_val - 3*std_val, color='0.4', linestyle='dotted', linewidth=2, label='-3 SD')

    mplt.title(f'Distribution of segmental {val}')
    mplt.xlabel(val)
    mplt.ylabel('Frequency')
    mplt.legend()
    mplt.show()

meanstd_val_df = pd.DataFrame(columns=['Measure', 'Mean', '3SD_lower', '3SD_upper'])
for idx, val in enumerate(["Mean","Median","StDev","Q1","Q3","Min","Max","ActVol","FAV","sCoGx","sCoGy","sCoGz", "COV CohD","Euc Dist", "Euc Distx", "Euc Disty", "Euc Distz"]):
    mean_val = noise_df_tmp2[val].mean()
    std_val = noise_df_tmp2[val].std()
    meanstd_val_df.loc[idx] = [val, mean_val,
                               mean_val - 3*std_val,
                               mean_val + 3*std_val]

# To display this raw string as a table in Jupyter:
noisenegtb = noise_df_tmp2.loc[noise_df_tmp2['Group'] == "Negative",["Group","Mean","Median","StDev","Q1","Q3","Min","Max","ActVol","FAV","sCoGx","sCoGy","sCoGz", "COV CohD","Euc Dist", "Euc Distx", "Euc Disty", "Euc Distz"]].describe().T
html_neg_tb = "<h3>Noise Only</h3>" + noisenegtb.to_html(index=True)

print("------ Summary Statistics for Noise Simulation ------")
HTML(html_neg_tb)

------ Summary Statistics for Noise Simulation ------

Euc Distx 109.0 0.061358 0.590362 -2.049000 -0.218000 0.045000 0.306000 2.018000

In [56]:
# Beta distribution fit for FAV in Noise Only SPM comparison

from scipy.stats import beta
from scipy.stats import norm

noise_df_tmp3 = noise_df_tmp2["FAV"].values



a_fit, b_fit, loc_fit, scale_fit = beta.fit(noise_df_tmp3, floc=0, fscale=1)


mplt.hist(noise_df_tmp3, bins=30, density=True, alpha=0.6, color = "seagreen")
x = np.linspace(min(noise_df_tmp3), max(noise_df_tmp3), 1000)
y = beta.pdf(x, a_fit, b_fit, loc_fit, scale_fit)
mplt.plot(x, y, 'g-', lw=2, label=f'Beta Fit (α={a_fit:.2f}, β={b_fit:.2f})')
mplt.title('Beta Distribution Fit of Noise-Only FAV Magnitudes')
mplt.xlabel('Fractional Activation Volume (FAV)')
mplt.ylabel('Density')
mplt.legend()
mplt.show()

num_sd = 3
pct = 1 - norm.sf(num_sd)
threshold = beta.ppf(pct, a_fit, b_fit, loc=0, scale=1)

print("------ Fitting a Beta Distribution to Segmental FAV from Noise Only Simulations  ------")
print(f"Fitted Alpha: {a_fit:.4f}")
print(f"Fitted Beta: {b_fit:.4f}")
print(f"Beta-based FAV Threshold ({round(100*pct,1)}th percentile): {threshold:.4f}")

import itertools
# Calculate all pairwise differences in your noise-only segments
diffs = [(a - b) for a, b in itertools.combinations(noise_df_tmp3, 2)]


from scipy.stats import laplace
loc_lap, scale_lap = laplace.fit(diffs)

mplt.hist(diffs, bins=30, density=True, alpha=0.6)
x = np.linspace(min(diffs), max(diffs), 1000)
mplt.plot(x, laplace.pdf(x, loc_lap, scale_lap), 'b-', lw=2, label='Laplace Fit')
mplt.title('Distribution of Signed FAV Differences (Noise Only)')
mplt.xlabel('FAV Difference (Segment A - Segment B)')
mplt.ylabel('Density')
mplt.legend()
mplt.show()

# For a two-tailed 99.7% confidence interval (0.15% in each tail)
upper_diff_threshold = laplace.ppf(0.9995, loc_lap, scale_lap)
lower_diff_threshold = laplace.ppf(0.0005, loc_lap, scale_lap)
print("------ Fitting a Laplace Distribution to Differences in Segmental FAV from Noise Only Simulations  ------")
print(f"Fitted Location: {loc_lap:.4f}")
print(f"Fitted Scale: {scale_lap:.4f}")
print(f"Laplace-based FAV Difference Threshold (99.9th percentile): +/- {upper_diff_threshold:.4f}")

------ Fitting a Beta Distribution to Segmental FAV from Noise Only Simulations  ------
Fitted Alpha: 3.3402
Fitted Beta: 92.4097
Beta-based FAV Threshold (99.9th percentile): 0.1157

------ Fitting a Laplace Distribution to Differences in Segmental FAV from Noise Only Simulations  ------
Fitted Location: 0.0000
Fitted Scale: 0.0186
Laplace-based FAV Difference Threshold (99.9th percentile): +/- 0.1283

Example 3: Application of segment labels to Cohen's D map of Mn(II)-enhancement.¶

We performed a paired t-test between post- and pre-Mn(II) images (n = 11), applied the optimal smoothing kernel, t-value threshold and cluster extent (see paper for details: kernel = 150 $\mu m$; T(10), p<0.05 $\ge$ 1.81; cluster extent $\ge$ 8 voxels), and converted the T-map to Cohen's D. This masked Cohen's D effect size map representing effect sizes of suprathreshold (statistically signficant) voxels was then 'segmented' using InVivoSegment. Below we present those results in relation to noise.

In [89]:
# Run GUI for statistical_tmap
# !python InVivoSegment.py --mode gui

# Note that we recommend running this in a separate terminal window, not as part of the Python kernel in a notebook as shown here. Example
In [60]:
# Load SPM segmentation CSV
spm_df = pd.read_csv("./examples/statistical_tmap/OutputData/CSVs/SPMResults.csv", na_values=["", " ", "NA", "-"])

# Tranform a few variables
## Center of Gravity of Signal from Segmental CoG
spm_df['Euc Dist']  = np.sqrt((spm_df['sCoGx']-spm_df['CoGx'])**2 + 
                              (spm_df['sCoGx']-spm_df['CoGx'])**2 + 
                              (spm_df['sCoGz']-spm_df['CoGz'])**2) * 0.1 
spm_df['Euc Distyz']  = np.sqrt((spm_df['sCoGx']-spm_df['CoGx'])**2 +
                                (spm_df['sCoGz']-spm_df['CoGz'])**2) * 0.1 
spm_df['Euc Distx'] = (spm_df['sCoGx']-spm_df['CoGx']) * 0.1 
spm_df['Euc Disty'] = (spm_df['sCoGy']-spm_df['CoGy']) * 0.1 
spm_df['Euc Distz'] = (spm_df['sCoGz']-spm_df['CoGz']) * 0.1
# converted to mm
for index, row in spm_df.iterrows():
    if row["sCoGx"] == 0 and row["sCoGy"] == 0 and row["sCoGz"] == 0:
        spm_df.at[index,"Euc Dist"] = 0


spm_df['COV CohD']   = spm_df['StDev'] / spm_df['Mean']

# Plot SPM Segmental Statistics
for val in meanstd_val_df["Measure"]:
    if val in ["Mean CohD", "ActVol"]:
        continue
    # plot_name = os.path.join("./examples/statistical_tmap/OutputData/ColumnGraphs",("SPM_" + val + "_T181-C8_Mean.png"))
    plot_name = os.path.join("./examples/statistical_tmap/OutputData/ColumnGraphs",("SPM_" + val + "_T181-C8_Beta99th.png"))
    spm_df_tmp = spm_df.copy()
    if val == "FAV":
        plt(
            seg_df = spm_df_tmp,
            seg_rm = seg_to_remove,
            order = "Grouping", 
            y = val,
            fill = "Condition",
            col_vals = ("#E34234", "#0000FF",
                        "#FFFF00", "#87CEEB"),
            save = False,
            fname = plot_name,
            fig_dims = dim_scale((3.3, 1.1), 2),
            font_scale = 2
            )
        fig = mplt.gcf()
        ax = mplt.gca()
        ax.axhline(y = threshold, color='0.2', linestyle='dotted', label=None)
        fig.savefig(plot_name, format='tiff', dpi=300, bbox_inches='tight')
    else:
        spm_df_tmp.loc[spm_df_tmp['FAV'] <= threshold, val] = 0
        plt(
            seg_df = spm_df_tmp,
            seg_rm = seg_to_remove,
            order = "Grouping", 
            y = val,
            fill = "Condition",
            col_vals = ("#E34234", "#0000FF",
                        "#FFFF00", "#87CEEB"),
            save = False,
            fname = plot_name,
            fig_dims = dim_scale((3.3, 1.1), 2),
            font_scale = 2
            )
        fig = mplt.gcf()
        ax = mplt.gca()
        # mmag = meanstd_val_df.loc[meanstd_val_df["Measure"] == val, "Mean"].values[0]
        # ax.axhline(y = mmag, color='0.2', linestyle='dotted', label=None)
        # mmagu = meanstd_val_df.loc[meanstd_val_df["Measure"] == val, "3SD_upper"].values[0]
        # # ax.axhline(y = mmagu, color='0.4', linestyle='dotted', label=None)
        fig.savefig(plot_name, format='tiff', dpi=300, bbox_inches='tight')

spm_df_tmp = spm_df.copy()
spm_df_tmp["ActVol"] = np.log10(spm_df_tmp["ActVol"]+1)
# plot_name = os.path.join("./examples/statistical_tmap/OutputData/ColumnGraphs",("SPM_ActVollog10_T181-C8.png"))
plot_name = os.path.join("./examples/statistical_tmap/OutputData/ColumnGraphs",("SPM_ActVollog10_T181-C8_Beta99th.png"))
spm_df_tmp.loc[spm_df_tmp['FAV'] <= threshold, "ActVol"] = 0
ax = plt(
    seg_df = spm_df_tmp,
    seg_rm = seg_to_remove,
    order = "Grouping", 
    y = "ActVol",
    fill = "Condition",
    col_vals = ("#E34234", "#0000FF",
                "#FFFF00", "#87CEEB"),
    save = False,
    fname = plot_name,
    fig_dims = dim_scale((3.3, 1.1), 2),
    font_scale = 2
    )
fig = mplt.gcf()
ax = mplt.gca()

# ax.axhline(y = np.log10(mmag), color='0.2', linestyle='dotted', label=None)
# mmagu = meanstd_val_df.loc[meanstd_val_df["Measure"] == "ActVol", "3SD_upper"].values[0]
# ax.axhline(y = np.log10(mmagu), color='0.4', linestyle='dotted', label=None)
fig.savefig(plot_name, format='tiff', dpi=300, bbox_inches='tight')
In [83]:
# determine how many segments survive at each threshold level

seg_ids = (spm_df["FAV"] > threshold) & (~spm_df["SegAbbr"].isin(seg_to_remove))
tot_segs = spm_df["FAV"][seg_ids].count()
print(f"Number of segments with FAV > {threshold:.4f}: {tot_segs}")

spm_df_filt = spm_df.loc[seg_ids,]
print(spm_df_filt["SegAbbr"][spm_df_filt["Mean"] == spm_df_filt["Mean"].max()])
print(spm_df_filt["SegAbbr"][spm_df_filt["COV CohD"] > 0.3].count())
print(spm_df_filt["SegAbbr"][spm_df_filt["Euc Dist"] == spm_df_filt["Euc Dist"].max()])

Number of segments with FAV > 0.1157: 69
57    MOBmi
Name: SegAbbr, dtype: object
16
70    BLA
Name: SegAbbr, dtype: object

Summary: Above we show segmental features one might extract from statistical maps, demonstrating the wide array of information one could use to characterize Mn(II) accumulations within and between regions. The column graphs have two dotted line: a black line that represent the average magnitude of that segmental measure from noise alone (from Example 2 above); and a gray line, representing the magnitude 3 SD above average. If the gray, 3SD line is not shown, it is out of the range of the plotted data.

Although not shown here, we also plotted the data using an FAV filtering (e.g., remove any segment with FAV <= 3SD FAV noise magnitude [~9%]). We named the output TIFFs with a "_FAVdenoise" suffix, and commented out lines 28 and 55 in the code chunk above, which were used to apply this filter.

In [10]:
# Correlations between Segmentation Statistics
import seaborn as sns

spm_df_filt = spm_df.loc[((spm_df["FAV"]>FAV_sdu) & 
                          (~spm_df["Domain"].isin(["V","WB","WM"]))),
                          ['Domain','SegAbbr','FAV', 'Mean', 'COV CohD']].reset_index(drop=True)

print(spm_df_filt["Domain"].unique())
# Create a PairGrid object and map different plots to different sections
g = sns.PairGrid(spm_df_filt[['FAV', 'Mean', 'COV CohD']])
g.map_diag(sns.histplot)
g.map_offdiag(sns.scatterplot, alpha=0)
g.add_legend()

# Custom function to draw text instead of points
def label_points(x, y, labels, **kwargs):
    ax = mplt.gca()
    for val_x, val_y, label in zip(x, y, labels):
        if val_x != val_y:
            ax.text(val_x, val_y, label, fontsize=9, ha='center', va='center')

# Map the function to the grid
g.map(label_points, labels = spm_df_filt['SegAbbr'])

['CB' 'HB' 'HYP' 'HIP' 'MB' 'STR/PAL' 'OLF' 'AMY' 'THA' 'CTX']
Out[10]:
<seaborn.axisgrid.PairGrid at 0x2173fcdee10>

Example 4: Intensity Maps of Post- and Pre-Mn(II) Brian Images¶

For this example we have the individual images (aligned but not smoothed) that were used as input for the SPM12 paired t-test in Example 3 above. In this case, images from multiple mice at two conditions have been segmented. Thus, we will plot the column graphs as the group average for a particular segmental statistic with error bars representing the standard error the mean. This result is not presented in the paper, but demonstrates how one would set up InVivo segmentation and graphical analysis of images from more complex experimental designs.

In [91]:
# Run GUI for longitudinal_cross-sectional
#!python InVivoSegment.py --mode gui
In [12]:
# Load Intensity segmentation CSV
intensity_df = pd.read_csv("./examples/intensities/OutputData/CSVs/IntensityResults.csv", na_values=["", " ", "NA", "-"])

intensity_df["COV"] = intensity_df["StDev"] / intensity_df["Mean"]

# Plot SPM Segmental Statistics
for val in ("Mean", "StDev", "COV"):
    plot_name = os.path.join("./examples/intensities/OutputData/ColumnGraphs",("Intensity_" + val + "_thrNone.png"))
    #print(plot_name)
    ax = plt(
        seg_df = intensity_df,
        seg_rm = seg_to_remove,
        order = "Grouping", 
        y = val,
        fill = "Condition",
        col_vals = ("#87CEEB", "#0000FF",
                    "#FFFF00", "#E34234", ),
        diff = None,
        save = True,
        fname = plot_name,
        fig_dims = dim_scale((3.3, 1.1), 2),
        font_scale = 2
        )
    
for val in ("Mean", "StDev", "COV"):
    # print(val)
    plot_name = os.path.join("./examples/intensities/OutputData/ColumnGraphs",("Intensity_" + val + "_thrNone.png"))
    #print(plot_name)
    ax = plt(
        seg_df = intensity_df,
        seg_rm = seg_to_remove,
        order = "Grouping", 
        y = val,
        fill = "Condition",
        col_vals = ("#E34234", "#0000FF",
                    "#FFFF00", "#87CEEB"),
        diff = "diff",
        save = True,
        fname = plot_name,
        fig_dims = dim_scale((3.3, 1.1), 2),
        font_scale = 2
        )

Summary: We show two types of graphs: 1) Graphs 1-3, group averages for segmental signal intensity measures for both pre-Mn(II) (light blue) and post-Mn(II) (dark blue); and 2) Graphs 4-6, group average fold change in these measures between pre- and post-Mn(II) conditions.

Note that a new option has been added to the column graph plotting function "diff = diff" to indicate a difference calculation should take place between conditions. Options include "diff" for difference (con2 - con1) or "fc" for fold change (100*(con2/con1 - 1)). Note: this will only work if there are exactly 2 conditions in the 'Condition' column of the dataframe and if y is a numerical variable. Additionally, the resulting plot will only show the computed differences for the second condition (con2). If more than 2 conditions are used, a subset should be created frp, the dataframe with only the two desired conditions.
